old_claim.note: Scientific Societies provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Learned and scientific societies did not enable the earliest printed compendia, but their journals, proceedings, and specialist contributor networks later scaled arts-and-sciences reference works.

source_shape.source_locator: Academies and Learned Societies exhibit: University of Chicago Library describes learned societies as a major force in disseminating scientific methods and documents academies publishing proceedings or transactions.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Learned societies disseminated scientific knowledge through institutional publication, while Britannica connects later encyclopaedias with specialist contributors and academy-linked arts-and-sciences works.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The sources support scientific societies as later scaling and contributor infrastructure for encyclopedic reference works, but not as a direct prerequisite for early printed compendia around 1500.

ontology_before: The old edge treated scientific societies as a generic enabling prerequisite for early printed encyclopedic compendia.
ontology_after: The corrected edge keeps the relationship but limits it to later scaling, publication, and specialist-contributor infrastructure for arts-and-sciences encyclopedias.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed edge type: scientific_societies is now a commercial_or_scaling_dependency rather than a direct enabling prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is later narrowed strictly to c.1500 printed compendia with no later arts-and-sciences encyclopedia scope.
A stronger source shows scientific societies directly enabled the first printed encyclopedic compendia.
The graph adds a narrower later encyclopedia node where the scientific-societies edge belongs instead.
short_reason: Scientific societies scale later arts-and-sciences encyclopedias but do not enable the earliest printed compendia.
